name: Standard Schnauzer
description: The Standard Schnauzer is the original Schnauzer and the progenitor of both the Giant and Miniature varieties, developed in Germany as a versatile farm dog for ratting, guarding, and general utility work, with records placing wire-coated dogs of this type in Bavarian households as far back as the 15th century. Highly intelligent, spirited, and strongly built, Standard Schnauzers are bold, confident dogs that can be stubborn but respond excellently to firm, consistent, reward-based training. Their low-shedding wiry coat requires hand-stripping or clipping every six to eight weeks to maintain proper texture and appearance.

conditions_detail.name: Hip Dysplasia
conditions_detail.description: Hip dysplasia is a genetic condition where the hip joint doesn't develop properly, causing the ball and socket to not fit together correctly. This leads to joint deterioration, pain, and arthritis over time.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Treatment ranges from weight management and physical therapy for mild cases to total hip replacement for severe cases. NSAIDs and joint supplements help manage pain.
prevention_tips: Screen breeding stock with hip scoring

conditions_detail.name: Epilepsy
conditions_detail.description: Epilepsy is the most common neurological disorder in dogs, characterised by recurrent seizures. Idiopathic (genetic) epilepsy is the most frequent form in young to middle-aged dogs with no underlying structural brain lesion.
conditions_detail.treatment_overview: Anticonvulsant drugs (phenobarbital, potassium bromide, levetiracetam) control seizures in the majority of cases. Treatment is lifelong. Cluster seizures and status epilepticus require emergency treatment.
prevention_tips: DNA testing available for several breeds
Avoid breeding affected dogs with idiopathic epilepsy
